Author: Shaw, David J.
Title: The lost first editions of Gabriel Meurier's Colloques ou nouvelle invention de propos familiers, printed by Plantin, 1556-7
Abstract Note: Abstract               Early in his career in Antwerp, Plantin printed a group of textbooks for the school-master Gabriel Meurier. Archival evidence from Plantin's business ledgers, from Meurier's privileges for sale of these books, and from a surviving printed fragment show that at least two previously unsuspected editions of one of these texts must have been printed. A summary of Meurier's career is given together with an analysis of the publication history of the texts covered by his privilege which were printed in Plantin's workshop.

Author: Kibbee, Douglas A.
Title: For to Speke Frenche Trewely: The French Language in England, 1000–1600: Its Status, Description, and Instruction
Abstract Note: The first grammatical descriptions of the French language were produced in England, several centuries before the first grammar written in French (but also several centuries after the Norman Conquest). This book describes the status of French in England during the period from the marriage of Emma of Normandy to thelred (1004) to the fixing of a (relatively) standard pedagogical scheme for the teaching of French of English speakers (ca. 1600). During this period French passed from a native language to a second language, became the official language of the legal profession, and ultimately fell back to a position of social accomplishment. At the same time, different pedagogical and descriptive traditions developed to meet these various needs. Here Kibbee traces the interaction of cultural, intellectual, social and technological history with the elaboration of a grammatical tradition. The book includes a bibliography and indexes of names, titles and subjects.
Series: Amsterdam Studies in the Theory and History of Linguistic Science. Series III, Studies in the History of the Language Sciences

Author: Hüllen, Werner
Title: Alle Sprachen nebeneinander. Die Anfänge des Fremdsprachenunterrichts in Europa (1450–1700)
Abstract Note: Zwei der ältesten Lehrbücher fremder Sprachen erlebten bis 1700 zehlreiche Neuauflagen, wobei zu den ursprünglichen zwei bis zu sechs weitere Sprachen hinzukamen. Die, aus diesen Erstausgaben entstandenen, großen Lehrbuchfamilien zeigen, dass die Einheit der europäischen Kultur mit der schwindenden Bedeutung des Lateinischen nicht verlorenging, sondern in der Vielfalt der nationalen Sprachen weiterlebte, die auf eine einheitliche Art und Weise gelernt wurden, und deren Lehre in gewisser Weise auch das Wissen der Zeit vermittelte.
